sourceMovie.title: 48 Hrs.
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Audiences latched onto 48 Hrs. (1982) as a crime theatrical experience: big enough for a group watch, specific enough to reward a second viewing once you know where it's headed. The crime and action blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Hot Fuzz
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If 48 Hrs. clicked for you, Hot Fuzz (2007) is a strong next stop: both trade in crime and action, with comic timing and sharper banter. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.title: Beverly Hills Cop
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If 48 Hrs. clicked for you, Beverly Hills Cop (1984) is a strong next stop: both trade in crime and action, with comic timing and sharper banter. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after 48 Hrs.?
faq.answer: Try Hot Fuzz, Beverly Hills Cop, Rush Hour 2—each shares DNA with 48 Hrs. while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is 48 Hrs.?
faq.answer: 48 Hrs. (1982) is best described as Crime, Action, Comedy. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is 48 Hrs. part of a franchise?
faq.answer: Yes—it's part of the "48 Hrs. Collection" storyline. You can enjoy it standalone, but sequels and tie-ins build on characters and continuity established here.
